title: Physics Puzzle — Joints, Forces & Level Goals
html:
Physics as the puzzle

In Asteroids you used RigidBody2D for arcade chaos. In a physics puzzle the simulation is the gameplay: the player nudges, drags, and connects bodies, then watches gravity, momentum, and joints carry a ball or box to a goal. Think Angry Birds, The Incredible Machine, or Crayon Physics. This lesson starts a brand-new genre and gives you the three tools that define it: impulses/forces, joints, and goal areas that detect a win.

Forces vs. impulses

A RigidBody2D is moved by the physics server, not by you setting position directly. You influence it with forces and impulses inside _physics_process or _integrate_forces:

  • Force (apply_central_force) is continuous, like wind or a rocket — applied every frame.
  • Impulse (apply_central_impulse) is instantaneous, like a kick or launch — applied once.
Rule.
Never set position or linear_velocity on a RigidBody every frame to move it — you fight the solver and get jitter. Use forces/impulses, or switch the body to freeze mode if you must teleport it.

Dragging a body with the mouse

A satisfying puzzle staple is grabbing a body and flinging it. We use a PinJoint2D-free approach: pull the body toward the mouse with a spring-like force while held.

draggable.gd
# Attach to a RigidBody2D with an Area2D child for click detection extends RigidBody2D var dragging := false const PULL := 40.0 const DAMP := 6.0 func _input(event): if event is InputEventMouseButton and event.button_index == MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT: if event.pressed and _mouse_over(): dragging = true elif not event.pressed: dragging = false func _mouse_over() -> bool: return get_global_mouse_position().distance_to(global_position) < 48.0 func _physics_process(delta): if dragging: var target = get_global_mouse_position() var to_target = target - global_position # spring toward mouse, damp existing velocity apply_central_force(to_target * PULL - linear_velocity * DAMP)

Joints connect bodies

Godot 2D has PinJoint2D (a hinge between two bodies), GrooveJoint2D (slides along a slot), and DampedSpringJoint2D (a stretchy spring). To build a swinging bridge or a catapult arm, drop a PinJoint2D in the scene and set its node_a and node_b to the two bodies' paths. Joints automatically pin the bodies at the joint's position.

spawn_chain.gd
# Build a rope of pinned links at runtime func build_chain(anchor: RigidBody2D, links: int): var prev := anchor for i in links: var link = preload('res://link.tscn').instantiate() link.position = anchor.position + Vector2(0, (i + 1) * 20) add_child(link) var joint = PinJoint2D.new() joint.position = link.position joint.node_a = prev.get_path() joint.node_b = link.get_path() add_child(joint) prev = link

The goal area and win detection

The objective is usually "get the ball into the cup." That is an Area2D with a body_entered signal, exactly like the signals you learned in Breakout. We require the ball to settle (low velocity) so it cannot just graze the goal.

goal.gd
extends Area2D signal level_won func _ready(): body_entered.connect(_on_body_entered) func _on_body_entered(body): if body.is_in_group('goal_ball'): # wait one moment, then check it stayed and slowed await get_tree().create_timer(0.4).timeout if overlaps_body(body) and body.linear_velocity.length() < 30.0: level_won.emit()
Lab.
Add a reset button that calls get_tree().reload_current_scene() so the player can retry instantly — fast retries are essential to physics-puzzle feel.

Level objectives, scored

Wrap each level in a small manager that tracks par (e.g., "win using 3 or fewer launches") and awards stars. Store par as an exported variable per level scene so designing a new level is just placing nodes and typing a number.

🎮 Build This: Physics Puzzle — Drop & Roll

This starts a new genre: a physics puzzle. The player drags planks and balls to roll a marble into a goal cup.

  1. Create a level scene with StaticBody2D walls/floor, a goal Area2D with the goal.gd script, and a marble RigidBody2D in group goal_ball.
  2. Add 2-3 draggable RigidBody2D planks using draggable.gd so the player can build a ramp.
  3. Add a launch zone: clicking empty space near the marble applies apply_central_impulse in the drag direction (track press and release points).
  4. Connect level_won to a manager that records launches used, compares to an exported par, and shows 1-3 stars.
  5. Add a swinging obstacle built from build_chain + a PinJoint2D anchor so timing matters.
  6. Wire a reset button and an arrow to the next level scene. Build three levels of rising difficulty.

title: RPG — Stats, Inventory, Dialogue & Turn-Based Combat
html:
The RPG is a systems game

An RPG is less about reflexes and more about data and systems: stats that grow, items you carry, NPCs who talk, and combat resolved by numbers. This is the perfect place to lean hard on the Resource types and class_name custom classes you learned early in the track. We start a new genre but reuse the top-down movement from your adventure game for the overworld.

Stats as a Resource

A Resource is a data container Godot can save, load, and edit in the inspector. Define a stats block once and reuse it for the player and every enemy.

stats.gd
class_name Stats extends Resource @export var max_hp := 30 @export var attack := 8 @export var defense := 3 @export var speed := 10 var hp: int func reset(): hp = max_hp func take_damage(raw: int) -> int: var dmg = max(1, raw - defense) hp = max(0, hp - dmg) return dmg func is_alive() -> bool: return hp > 0

Now create concrete .tres files: Hero.tres, Slime.tres, each with its own numbers, all of type Stats. Designers tune the game without touching code.

Items and inventory

Items are also Resources. An inventory is just an array of them plus quantities.

item.gd
class_name Item extends Resource @export var id := '' @export var name := 'Potion' @export var heal := 0 @export var icon: Texture2D
inventory.gd (autoload)
extends Node signal changed var slots: Dictionary = {} # item_id -> {item, qty} func add(item: Item, qty := 1): if slots.has(item.id): slots[item.id].qty += qty else: slots[item.id] = {'item': item, 'qty': qty} changed.emit() func use(item_id: String, target: Stats) -> bool: if not slots.has(item_id): return false var item: Item = slots[item_id].item target.hp = min(target.max_hp, target.hp + item.heal) slots[item_id].qty -= 1 if slots[item_id].qty <= 0: slots.erase(item_id) changed.emit() return true

Build the inventory UI from Control nodes — a GridContainer of TextureButtons. Listen to the changed signal and rebuild the grid. This is the decoupled, signal-driven pattern from Breakout applied to UI.

A data-driven dialogue system

Store conversations as an array of lines with optional branching. Keep it simple: each line has speaker text, and the final line can present choices.

dialogue.gd
extends CanvasLayer signal finished @onready var label = $Panel/Label var _lines: Array = [] var _i := 0 func start(lines: Array): _lines = lines _i = 0 visible = true _show() func _show(): label.text = _lines[_i] func _unhandled_input(e): if visible and e.is_action_pressed('ui_accept'): _i += 1 if _i >= _lines.size(): visible = false finished.emit() else: _show()

The turn-based combat loop

Combat is a state machine over turns: build a turn order by speed, let each combatant act, apply results, check for death, repeat. Use await to pause for animations and player input.

battle.gd
func run_battle(hero: Stats, foe: Stats): hero.reset(); foe.reset() while hero.is_alive() and foe.is_alive(): var action = await get_player_choice() # 'attack' or 'item' if action == 'attack': foe.take_damage(hero.attack) if foe.is_alive(): hero.take_damage(foe.attack) # enemy turn update_bars() return hero.is_alive()
Design law.
Keep combat readable: show exact damage numbers and HP bars. Players forgive hard fights; they do not forgive not understanding why they lost.
🎮 Build This: RPG — Mini Quest

This starts a new genre: an RPG, reusing the top-down overworld movement from your earlier adventure game.

  1. Make a Stats resource and author Hero.tres plus two enemy .tres files.
  2. Add an Inventory autoload and an inventory UI grid that updates from the changed signal.
  3. Place an NPC that triggers dialogue.start([...]) on interact; on finished, grant a quest and a potion via Inventory.add.
  4. Place enemies in the overworld; touching one loads the battle scene with that enemy's Stats.
  5. Implement the turn-based loop with Attack and Item buttons; using a potion calls Inventory.use.
  6. On victory, award XP and return to the overworld; on defeat, respawn at a save point.

title: Game AI — FSM, A* Pathfinding, NavMesh & Behavior Trees
html:
Making enemies think

So far your foes moved in straight lines or simple patterns. This concept lesson gives them brains. We cover three layers of AI you will retrofit into the games you have already built: finite state machines (FSM) for behavior, pathfinding (A* and NavigationServer) for movement, and behavior trees for complex, reusable decision-making.

The finite state machine

An FSM is a small set of states (Idle, Patrol, Chase, Attack) with rules for switching between them. The cleanest Godot pattern uses one node per state as children of a StateMachine node, each implementing enter, update, and physics_update.

state_machine.gd
class_name StateMachine extends Node @export var start_state: NodePath var current: State func _ready(): for c in get_children(): c.machine = self current = get_node(start_state) current.enter() func _physics_process(delta): current.physics_update(delta) func transition_to(name: String): if has_node(name): current.exit() current = get_node(name) current.enter()
state_chase.gd
class_name ChaseState extends State func physics_update(delta): var player = machine.owner.player machine.owner.move_toward_point(player.global_position, delta) if machine.owner.global_position.distance_to(player.global_position) > 400: machine.transition_to('Patrol')

A* on a grid

For grid-based games (your top-down dungeon), Godot ships AStarGrid2D. Mark walls as solid and ask for a path between two cells.

astar_grid.gd
var grid := AStarGrid2D.new() func setup(size: Vector2i, cell := 32): grid.region = Rect2i(Vector2i.ZERO, size) grid.cell_size = Vector2(cell, cell) grid.update() func block(cell: Vector2i): grid.set_point_solid(cell, true) func path(from: Vector2i, to: Vector2i) -> PackedVector2Array: return grid.get_point_path(from, to)

NavigationServer and NavMesh

For free-form 2D or 3D movement, use a navigation mesh. Add a NavigationRegion2D (or 3D), bake the geometry, then give each agent a NavigationAgent2D child. The agent computes paths and avoids other agents.

nav_enemy.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @onready var agent: NavigationAgent2D = $NavigationAgent2D @export var speed := 120.0 var target: Node2D func _physics_process(delta): if target: agent.target_position = target.global_position if agent.is_navigation_finished(): return var next = agent.get_next_path_position() velocity = global_position.direction_to(next) * speed move_and_slide()
Tip.
Call agent.target_position at most a few times per second (via a timer), not every frame, on large maps — repathing is the costliest AI operation.

Behavior trees

FSMs explode in complexity as behaviors grow (an N-state machine has up to N×N transitions). A behavior tree scales better: it is a tree of composite nodes (Sequence, Selector) and leaf nodes (Conditions, Actions). Each tick returns SUCCESS, FAILURE, or RUNNING.

  • Selector tries children until one succeeds ("do this OR that").
  • Sequence runs children until one fails ("do this THEN that").
bt.gd
enum {SUCCESS, FAILURE, RUNNING} class Selector: var children := [] func tick(actor, delta): for c in children: var r = c.tick(actor, delta) if r != FAILURE: return r return FAILURE class Sequence: var children := [] func tick(actor, delta): for c in children: var r = c.tick(actor, delta) if r != SUCCESS: return r return SUCCESS

A guard's tree might read: Selector [ Sequence [CanSeePlayer? → Chase → Attack], Patrol ]. The same leaf nodes drop into any enemy, which is why studios prefer behavior trees for ambitious AI.

Law.
Choose the simplest tool: FSM for 3-5 states, A*/NavMesh for movement, behavior trees only when behavior logic genuinely branches. Over-engineering AI is a classic time sink.
🎮 Build This: AI Retrofit — Smarter Foes

This is a concept lesson that extends prior games (your top-down adventure and twin-stick shooter) by upgrading their enemies.

  1. Add the StateMachine + state-node pattern to an existing enemy with Idle, Patrol, Chase, and Attack states.
  2. Give the dungeon enemy AStarGrid2D pathfinding so it routes around walls instead of bumping them.
  3. Convert an open-area enemy to NavigationAgent2D with a baked NavigationRegion2D; throttle repathing with a timer.
  4. Implement the behavior-tree classes and rebuild one enemy's brain as Selector/Sequence leaves.
  5. Add a vision check (raycast to player) as the condition that flips Patrol into Chase.

title: Strategy — Tower Defense with Navigation & Waves
html:
A genre built on systems you already know

Tower defense is the perfect strategy starter: it combines the pathfinding from the AI lesson, the wave spawning from your shooter, and the signal-driven economy patterns from your RPG. The player places towers along a path; enemies walk from spawn to base; towers auto-target and shoot; money earned from kills buys more towers. This starts a new genre but stitches together everything you have learned.

Enemies that follow the path

Use a NavigationAgent2D (or a baked Path2D with PathFollow2D for fixed lanes). For a single fixed lane, PathFollow2D is simplest and deterministic.

creep.gd
# Root is PathFollow2D, child Sprite2D + Area2D; placed under a Path2D extends PathFollow2D @export var speed := 80.0 @export var max_hp := 20 @export var bounty := 5 var hp: int signal died(bounty) signal reached_base func _ready(): hp = max_hp func _physics_process(delta): progress += speed * delta if progress_ratio >= 1.0: reached_base.emit() queue_free() func hurt(dmg: int): hp -= dmg if hp <= 0: died.emit(bounty) queue_free()

Targeting AI for towers

A tower needs a detection Area2D (its range) and a targeting policy. The classic policy is "first" (the creep furthest along the path), which prevents leaks. Track creeps in range and pick by progress_ratio.

tower.gd
extends Node2D @onready var range_area = $Range @onready var cooldown = $Cooldown # Timer @export var damage := 6 var in_range: Array = [] func _ready(): range_area.body_entered.connect(func(b): in_range.append(b)) range_area.body_exited.connect(func(b): in_range.erase(b)) cooldown.timeout.connect(_fire) func _pick_target(): in_range = in_range.filter(func(c): return is_instance_valid(c)) if in_range.is_empty(): return null in_range.sort_custom(func(a, b): return a.progress_ratio > b.progress_ratio) return in_range[0] func _fire(): var t = _pick_target() if t: look_at(t.global_position) t.hurt(damage)
Tip.
Set the tower's Range Area2D collision mask to detect only the creep layer, and the creep's Area2D to that same layer. Clean layers prevent towers from targeting projectiles or each other.

Placement with a build grid

Let the player drop towers on valid tiles. Snap the mouse to a grid and block placement on the path or occupied cells.

builder.gd
const CELL := 64 var occupied := {} func _unhandled_input(e): if e is InputEventMouseButton and e.pressed: var cell = (get_global_mouse_position() / CELL).floor() if occupied.has(cell) or _on_path(cell): return if Economy.spend(50): var tower = preload('res://tower.tscn').instantiate() tower.position = cell * CELL + Vector2(CELL, CELL) / 2 add_child(tower) occupied[cell] = true

Economy and wave manager

An autoload tracks gold and lives; the wave manager spawns creeps over time and connects their signals.

waves.gd
@export var path: Path2D var waves := [{'count': 8, 'gap': 0.8}, {'count': 14, 'gap': 0.5}] func run(): for wave in waves: for i in wave.count: _spawn() await get_tree().create_timer(wave.gap).timeout await get_tree().create_timer(4.0).timeout # breather func _spawn(): var c = preload('res://creep.tscn').instantiate() c.died.connect(func(b): Economy.add(b)) c.reached_base.connect(func(): Economy.lose_life()) path.add_child(c)
Strategy law.
Balance is the whole game. Tune three dials together: creep HP/wave, tower DPS/cost, and gold/kill. A spreadsheet beats guessing — author waves as data so you can rebalance without code.
🎮 Build This: Tower Defense — Hold the Line

This starts a new genre: strategy/tower defense, reusing wave spawning from your shooter and pathfinding from the AI lesson.

  1. Draw a Path2D from spawn to base; implement creep.gd as a PathFollow2D.
  2. Build a tower with a range Area2D, a cooldown Timer, and the "first" targeting policy.
  3. Add an Economy autoload (gold + lives) with spend, add, and lose_life signals to the HUD.
  4. Implement grid placement that blocks the path and charges gold per tower.
  5. Drive waves from a data array; award bounty on kill, drain a life on leak.
  6. Add a second tower type (slow vs. high-damage) and a win/lose screen.

title: Procedural Generation — Endless Runner & a Rhythm Game
html:
Two genres, one theme: time

This lesson builds two small games that share a theme. The endless runner teaches procedural generation via chunk spawning and recycling. The rhythm game teaches precise timing against an audio clock. Both start new genres; both reuse the platformer movement and Control-node UI you already have.

Endless runner: chunked procedural levels

An endless runner never ends, so you cannot pre-build the level. Instead you spawn chunks (pre-made scene segments) ahead of the player and free chunks behind. The world scrolls by moving the camera/player forward; chunks are recycled.

chunk_spawner.gd
extends Node2D @export var chunks: Array[PackedScene] @export var player: Node2D var next_x := 0.0 const CHUNK_W := 960.0 var active: Array = [] func _process(_d): # keep ~3 chunks ahead of the player while next_x < player.global_position.x + CHUNK_W * 3: _spawn() # recycle chunks well behind the player for c in active.duplicate(): if c.global_position.x < player.global_position.x - CHUNK_W: active.erase(c) c.queue_free() func _spawn(): var c = chunks.pick_random().instantiate() c.position.x = next_x add_child(c) active.append(c) next_x += CHUNK_W
Tip.
For fairness, hand-author each chunk so it is always beatable, then randomize the order. Fully random tile placement produces impossible jumps. Seed the RNG with seed() if you want shareable runs.

Difficulty ramps by tagging chunks with a difficulty value and only allowing harder chunks as score climbs. The player itself reuses your CharacterBody2D jump code with auto-run: horizontal velocity is constant, the player only controls the jump.

Rhythm game: syncing to audio

The hard truth of rhythm games: never trust frame time for timing. Use the audio playback position as the master clock, because audio and video can drift. Godot exposes AudioStreamPlayer.get_playback_position() plus AudioServer.get_time_since_last_mix() and get_output_latency() for sub-frame accuracy.

conductor.gd
extends AudioStreamPlayer @export var bpm := 120.0 var sec_per_beat: float var song_time := 0.0 var beat := 0 signal on_beat(n) func _ready(): sec_per_beat = 60.0 / bpm play() func _process(_d): # precise song position accounting for mix + latency song_time = get_playback_position() \ + AudioServer.get_time_since_last_mix() \ - AudioServer.get_output_latency() var b = int(song_time / sec_per_beat) if b > beat: beat = b on_beat.emit(beat)

Beatmaps and scoring windows

A beatmap is just an array of times (in beats or seconds) when a note should be hit. Spawn note sprites to arrive at the hit line exactly on their beat, then score the player's input against the nearest note's time.

judge.gd
const PERFECT := 0.05 const GOOD := 0.12 func judge(note_time: float, song_time: float) -> String: var diff = abs(note_time - song_time) if diff <= PERFECT: return 'PERFECT' elif diff <= GOOD: return 'GOOD' elif diff <= 0.20: return 'OK' return 'MISS'

On each input action, find the un-judged note with the closest target time, call judge, award points and combo, and despawn that note. Notes that scroll past the line uncaught count as a miss and break the combo.

Law.
Calibrate. Offer the player a one-time latency offset slider; every display and audio setup is different, and a fixed offset added to song_time turns a frustrating chart into a fair one.
🎮 Build This: Runner + Rhythm Pack

This starts two new genres: an endless runner and a rhythm game, reusing platformer movement and Control UI.

  1. Author 4-5 beatable chunk scenes; build the recycling spawner that keeps three ahead and frees those behind.
  2. Give the runner auto-run + jump (your platformer code), a score that climbs with distance, and a death-on-hit reset.
  3. Ramp difficulty by gating harder chunks behind score thresholds.
  4. Build the Conductor with accurate song position; emit on_beat.
  5. Spawn notes from a beatmap array timed to arrive at the hit line; implement the judge windows, combo, and a results screen.
  6. Add a calibration offset slider that shifts song_time.

title: Fighting Game & Procedural Roguelike
html:
Two demanding genres

This lesson pairs a 1v1 fighting game (precise state machines and hitboxes) with a procedural roguelike (dungeon generation and permadeath). Both start new genres; the fighter reuses your AnimationTree/state-machine knowledge and the roguelike reuses top-down combat and the AI from earlier lessons.

Fighting game: states and frame data

A fighter is a strict state machine: Idle, Walk, Jump, Attack, Block, Hit-stun. Crucially, attacks have frame data — startup (windup), active (hitbox live), and recovery (vulnerable). This is why fighters feel deliberate; you cannot cancel arbitrarily.

fighter.gd
extends CharacterBody2D enum S {IDLE, WALK, JUMP, ATTACK, HIT} var state := S.IDLE var hp := 100 @onready var hitbox: Area2D = $Hitbox @onready var anim: AnimationPlayer = $Anim func attack(): if state in [S.ATTACK, S.HIT]: return # commit, no cancel state = S.ATTACK anim.play('jab') # anim toggles hitbox on active frames await anim.animation_finished state = S.IDLE func take_hit(dmg: int, knockback: Vector2): state = S.HIT hp -= dmg velocity = knockback anim.play('hitstun') await anim.animation_finished if state == S.HIT: state = S.IDLE

Hitboxes and hurtboxes

Separate the hitbox (the offensive Area2D, enabled only on active frames) from the hurtbox (the vulnerable Area2D). Enable the hitbox via Call Method tracks in the AnimationPlayer so it is live for exactly the right frames.

hitbox.gd
extends Area2D @export var damage := 10 @export var owner_fighter: Node func _ready(): monitoring = false # off until an active frame enables it area_entered.connect(_on_hurtbox) func _on_hurtbox(hurt: Area2D): var target = hurt.get_parent() if target == owner_fighter: return var dir = sign(target.global_position.x - owner_fighter.global_position.x) target.take_hit(damage, Vector2(dir * 250, -120)) monitoring = false # one hit per swing
Tip.
Add a few frames of hit-stop (freeze both fighters briefly on contact) with Engine.time_scale or a manual pause. Nothing sells impact like a 4-frame freeze on a clean hit.

Roguelike: procedural dungeon generation

A roguelike generates a fresh dungeon each run. The simplest robust method is random walk / room placement: scatter non-overlapping rooms, then connect their centers with corridors.

dungeon.gd
var rng := RandomNumberGenerator.new() var rooms: Array[Rect2i] = [] func generate(count: int, bounds: Vector2i): rooms.clear() for i in count: var size = Vector2i(rng.randi_range(4, 9), rng.randi_range(4, 9)) var pos = Vector2i(rng.randi_range(1, bounds.x - size.x - 1), rng.randi_range(1, bounds.y - size.y - 1)) var r = Rect2i(pos, size) if rooms.any(func(o): return o.grow(1).intersects(r)): continue rooms.append(r) _carve_rooms() _connect_rooms() func _connect_rooms(): for i in range(1, rooms.size()): var a = rooms[i - 1].get_center() var b = rooms[i].get_center() _carve_corridor(a, b) # L-shaped tunnel via TileMapLayer

Paint walls/floors into a TileMapLayer from the generated rects. Seed rng with a run seed so the same seed reproduces a dungeon — invaluable for debugging and for daily-challenge runs.

Permadeath and meta-progression

Permadeath means one death ends the run and resets per-run state. But great roguelikes keep meta-progression: unlocks that persist across runs. Separate two save scopes: ephemeral run state (HP, items this run) versus persistent meta state (unlocked characters, currency).

Law.
Variety beats balance in roguelikes. A wide pool of items/enemies that combine in surprising ways makes runs feel fresh; perfect balance with few options gets stale fast.
🎮 Build This: Fighter + Roguelike Pack

This starts two new genres: a 1v1 fighting game and a procedural roguelike, reusing AnimationTree state machines and top-down combat/AI.

  1. Build two fighters with the Idle/Walk/Jump/Attack/Hit state machine and separate hit/hurtboxes driven by AnimationPlayer method tracks.
  2. Add startup/active/recovery via the animation timeline, knockback, hit-stop, and a best-of-3 round manager with health bars.
  3. Give player 2 a simple AI (approach + attack on range), reusing the FSM lesson.
  4. For the roguelike, implement room-scatter + corridor generation painted into a TileMapLayer from a run seed.
  5. Populate rooms with enemies (reuse AI), item pickups, and an exit to the next generated floor.
  6. Implement permadeath: split ephemeral run state from a persistent meta-save that unlocks new starting gear.

title: Shading Language, GPUParticles & Game-Feel VFX
html:
Polish is a skill, not luck

The difference between a prototype and a game people feel is juice: shaders, particles, screen shake, and hit-stop. This concept lesson teaches Godot's shading language and the VFX nodes, then shows how to layer game-feel across the portfolio you have built.

Godot's shading language

Godot shaders are GLSL-like. A shader_type canvas_item shader runs per-pixel on 2D nodes; spatial runs on 3D meshes. The fragment() function sets COLOR for each pixel. Uniforms are parameters you set from GDScript.

flash.gdshader
shader_type canvas_item; // flash a sprite white when hit; amount 0..1 from script uniform float flash_amount : hint_range(0.0, 1.0) = 0.0; uniform vec4 flash_color : source_color = vec4(1.0); void fragment() { vec4 tex = texture(TEXTURE, UV); COLOR = mix(tex, vec4(flash_color.rgb, tex.a), flash_amount * tex.a); }
hit_flash.gd
# Trigger the shader from the game func flash(): var mat: ShaderMaterial = sprite.material var tw = create_tween() mat.set_shader_parameter('flash_amount', 1.0) tw.tween_method(func(v): mat.set_shader_parameter('flash_amount', v), 1.0, 0.0, 0.15)

A 3D dissolve effect

Spatial shaders let you do things textures cannot, like dissolving an enemy on death using a noise texture and discard.

dissolve.gdshader
shader_type spatial; uniform sampler2D noise; uniform float threshold : hint_range(0.0, 1.0) = 0.0; uniform sampler2D albedo : source_color; void fragment() { float n = texture(noise, UV).r; if (n < threshold) discard; // burn away pixels below threshold ALBEDO = texture(albedo, UV).rgb; }

GPUParticles

Use GPUParticles2D/GPUParticles3D for thousands of cheap particles simulated on the GPU. Configure them with a ParticleProcessMaterial: emission shape, velocity, gravity, scale-over-life, color ramp. For one-shot bursts (an explosion), set one_shot = true and call restart().

burst.gd
func explode_at(pos: Vector2): var p: GPUParticles2D = preload('res://explosion.tscn').instantiate() p.global_position = pos p.one_shot = true add_child(p) p.restart() p.emitting = true # free after lifetime so it cleans itself up p.finished.connect(p.queue_free)

Screen shake and hit-stop

Two cheap effects punch far above their cost. Screen shake jitters the camera offset for a moment; hit-stop briefly freezes time on impact.

juice.gd (on Camera2D)
var shake := 0.0 func add_shake(amount: float): shake = min(shake + amount, 1.0) func _process(delta): if shake > 0: offset = Vector2(randf_range(-1, 1), randf_range(-1, 1)) * shake * 12.0 shake = max(0, shake - delta * 2.0) else: offset = Vector2.ZERO func hit_stop(duration := 0.06): Engine.time_scale = 0.0 await get_tree().create_timer(duration, true, false, true).timeout Engine.time_scale = 1.0
Tip.
The fourth arg to create_timer (ignore_time_scale = true) lets the hit-stop timer keep ticking while time_scale is 0, so it can unfreeze itself.
Game-feel law.
Juice the moment of impact: combine a white flash, a particle burst, a 4-6 frame hit-stop, and a small camera shake on every meaningful hit. Subtle and layered beats one loud effect.
🎮 Build This: VFX Pass — Juice Everything

This is a concept lesson that extends your whole portfolio by adding a polish layer to games you have already built.

  1. Add the hit-flash shader to enemies in your shooter and adventure game; trigger it on damage.
  2. Add the 3D dissolve shader to dying enemies in your FPS/TPS.
  3. Replace instant deaths with a GPUParticles burst that frees itself.
  4. Add a shake-capable Camera2D autoload helper and call add_shake on explosions and big hits.
  5. Add hit_stop to your fighting game and any heavy melee hit.
  6. Layer all four effects on one signature attack and compare before/after.

title: Simulation/Management Game, Saving & Data-Driven Design
html:
Games made of spreadsheets

Simulation and management games — colony sims, tycoons, farm games — run on data-driven design and a tick-based economy. There is little twitch input; the joy is watching numbers compound from your decisions. This starts a new genre and leans on the Resource system from your RPG, plus a proper save/load system you can reuse everywhere.

Data-driven definitions

Define building and resource types as Resources so designers add content by creating .tres files, never editing code.

building_def.gd
class_name BuildingDef extends Resource @export var id := '' @export var name := 'Farm' @export var cost := {'gold': 50} # per-tick production and consumption @export var produces := {'food': 2} @export var consumes := {'water': 1}

The economy tick

Simulations advance on a fixed tick, not per-frame, so the sim runs the same regardless of framerate and can be sped up. Use a Timer to fire ticks; each tick, every building produces and consumes.

economy.gd (autoload)
extends Node signal tick_done(resources) var resources := {'gold': 200, 'food': 10, 'water': 20} var buildings: Array[BuildingDef] = [] func _ready(): var t = Timer.new() t.wait_time = 1.0 t.timeout.connect(do_tick) add_child(t); t.start() func do_tick(): for b in buildings: # only run if inputs are available if _can_afford(b.consumes): _apply(b.consumes, -1) _apply(b.produces, 1) tick_done.emit(resources) func _apply(d: Dictionary, sign: int): for k in d: resources[k] = resources.get(k, 0) + d[k] * sign func _can_afford(d: Dictionary) -> bool: for k in d: if resources.get(k, 0) < d[k]: return false return true
Tip.
Decouple tick rate from a speed control by adjusting the Timer.wait_time (1x, 2x, 3x). Because the sim is tick-based, fast-forward is free and deterministic.

Saving with Resources

Godot can serialize a Resource straight to disk with ResourceSaver and load it with ResourceLoader. This is ideal for structured save data because types are preserved.

save_resource.gd
class_name SaveGame extends Resource @export var resources := {} @export var building_ids: Array[String] = [] @export var day := 1 # --- saving --- func write(): ResourceSaver.save(self, 'user://save.tres') static func read() -> SaveGame: if ResourceLoader.exists('user://save.tres'): return ResourceLoader.load('user://save.tres') return SaveGame.new()
Security caveat.
ResourceLoader.load on a .tres can run embedded scripts. For untrusted/cloud saves, load with the safe flag or use JSON instead. For local single-player saves it is fine.

Saving with JSON

JSON is portable, human-readable, and safe for untrusted data. Convert your state to a Dictionary and use JSON.stringify / JSON.parse_string. Always write to user://, the per-user writable directory.

save_json.gd
func save_json(data: Dictionary): var f = FileAccess.open('user://save.json', FileAccess.WRITE) f.store_string(JSON.stringify(data, ' ')) f.close() func load_json() -> Dictionary: if not FileAccess.file_exists('user://save.json'): return {} var f = FileAccess.open('user://save.json', FileAccess.READ) var result = JSON.parse_string(f.get_as_text()) f.close() return result if result is Dictionary else {}
Data law.
Separate definitions (immutable content authored as Resources) from state (the changing save data). Save only state and reference definitions by id. This keeps saves small and lets you patch content without breaking old saves.
🎮 Build This: Simulation — Tiny Colony

This starts a new genre: a simulation/management sandbox, reusing the Resource system from your RPG.

  1. Author 3-4 BuildingDef resources (Farm, Well, Mine, Market) with cost/produces/consumes.
  2. Build a placement UI that spends resources and adds the building's def to the Economy.
  3. Run the tick loop; update a resource HUD from the tick_done signal; add 1x/2x/3x speed controls.
  4. Add a fail/win condition (food hits zero = starvation; reach a gold goal = win).
  5. Implement Save/Load both ways: a SaveGame Resource and a JSON path, storing state by building id.
  6. Reload the game and confirm the colony restores exactly — that round-trip is the real test.
